title: Chain-of-Thought Unlock
todo: Take a real prioritisation decision you're working on. Ask Claude once normally. Then ask the exact same question again, adding only: 'Think through this step by step before answering.' Paste both responses side by side and compare the depth and structure.
why: Adding one phrase activates a fundamentally different reasoning mode — the model deliberates rather than pattern-matches.
learn: How to unlock structured reasoning versus surface-level responses from any LLM, and when each mode is appropriate.
surprise: The same model, same question, one extra phrase — the second answer is often board-level quality. Most people never try this and assume all Claude responses are the same quality.
tip: Use a real, open decision you're sitting on — the more specific the context, the more dramatic the difference between the two outputs.

title: Few-Shot Style Teaching
todo: Find two examples of a written format you love — a crisp PRD section, a sharp Slack update, a tight strategy memo. Paste both into Claude with zero instructions. Then ask it to produce a third piece in the same style for a new topic you choose.
why: You can teach a model your preferred format in 30 seconds without templates, prompt engineering jargon, or fine-tuning.
learn: The mechanics of few-shot prompting and why examples beat instructions for controlling output style.
surprise: Claude reverse-engineers your style from examples more accurately than it follows explicit instructions. Most people write long 'please write like X' prompts when two examples work better.
tip: Paste both examples first with a line break between them, then on a new line just say 'Now write one for: [your topic].' No additional instructions needed.

title: The Assumption Audit
todo: Generate any output from Claude — a PRD section, a strategy summary, a user story. Then immediately follow up with: 'Now list every assumption you made in creating that response. Flag which assumptions are most likely to be wrong for a SaaS product context.'
why: AI outputs look authoritative but are built on invisible assumptions about your context, industry, and users.
learn: How to interrogate AI reasoning rather than passively consuming outputs — the single most important habit for working safely with AI.
surprise: Claude will surface 5–8 assumptions you accepted without noticing. This one follow-up permanently changes how you review every AI output you ever produce.
tip: Do this on a piece of output you already generated and found useful — auditing something you liked is more instructive than auditing something obviously bad.

title: Persona Framing Comparison
todo: Pick a real product question you have right now. Ask it twice in two separate Claude conversations. First: plain question. Second: prefix with 'You are a skeptical VP of Engineering at a Series B SaaS company who has watched too many PM initiatives fail in production.' Compare what changes.
why: Identity framing changes the depth, tone, and what the model chooses to challenge — it's a first-class input, not a trick.
learn: How system-level context shapes model behaviour and how to use persona framing deliberately to get the perspective you actually need.
surprise: The second answer will push back on things the first answer confidently affirmed. The same model becomes a genuinely different thinking partner with a 15-word prefix.
tip: Open two separate browser tabs to keep the conversations clean — don't ask both versions in the same thread.

title: The Format Control Sprint
todo: Take one product decision you recently made. In a single Claude conversation, ask it to communicate that decision in four formats: a 3-sentence Slack message, a one-page exec summary, a 5-bullet risk list, and a 2-column decision table. Do not change any context between requests.
why: The same information repackaged for four audiences usually takes a PM 2–3 hours. This collapses it to under 5 minutes.
learn: Output format is a first-class variable in prompting — naming it explicitly produces dramatically better results than hoping Claude guesses the right format.
surprise: All four outputs are usable in under 60 seconds. The moment this clicks, you'll stop spending afternoons reformatting the same ideas for different stakeholders.
tip: Write your decision in one clear paragraph first, then start the formatting requests — giving Claude a solid base makes every format sharper.

title: Iterative Narrowing Chain
todo: Start with: 'Give me 20 feature ideas for [your product].' When done, say: 'Cut to the top 5, with one-line justifications for each.' When done, say: 'Write acceptance criteria for number 1 only.' Notice how quality compounds across steps.
why: Chaining short focused prompts outperforms one large prompt for complex, multi-step decisions.
learn: How to structure multi-turn conversations to get progressively sharper outputs — one of the highest-leverage AI workflow skills.
surprise: The acceptance criteria from a chained conversation are substantially more specific than if you'd asked for them directly. The narrowing steps do real cognitive work.
tip: Be specific about your product in the very first prompt — 'a B2B project management tool for construction teams' beats 'a project management tool.'

title: PRD in 10 Minutes
todo: Describe a feature you're currently working on in exactly 3 sentences. Ask Claude to generate a full PRD: problem statement, user stories, edge cases, success metrics, and open questions. Read the edge cases section first — that's where the value is.
why: A first-draft PRD that takes 4 hours to write from scratch takes 8 minutes with AI. The edge cases section alone is worth the exercise.
learn: How to use AI as a first-draft accelerator and what a good PRD prompt looks like versus a vague one.
surprise: The edge cases Claude surfaces will include at least 2 things your team hasn't formally discussed. The open questions section will name assumptions your engineering team would have surfaced in sprint planning.
prereqs: A feature idea or brief to work from
tip: Write your 3 sentences before opening Claude — specificity in the input is the single biggest driver of quality in the output.

title: The Hole-Finder
todo: Paste your current PRD or feature spec into Claude. Prompt: 'Read this spec and identify: (1) where I am being vague, (2) where I am over-optimistic about complexity, (3) what edge cases I have not considered. Be direct and specific — do not soften the feedback.'
why: Most PRD reviews from colleagues are too polite. Claude is not. It will find real gaps.
learn: How to use AI as a pre-review QA pass before sharing with engineering — catching ambiguities before the spec review saves more time than any other single habit.
surprise: Engineering teams that get specs pre-reviewed with this prompt report shorter spec reviews and fewer mid-sprint scope questions. The feedback is almost always right.
prereqs: An existing PRD or feature spec to review
tip: Paste the entire spec, not just sections — Claude finds inconsistencies across the document that section-by-section review misses.

title: Acceptance Criteria Factory
todo: Take one user story from your current sprint. Ask Claude for 8 acceptance criteria. Then, in the same conversation, ask: 'Now give me 3 acceptance criteria I almost certainly forgot.' Focus specifically on what appears in that second response.
why: Standard AC generation is good. The 'what you forgot' prompt targets blind spots — failure states, empty states, permission edge cases.
learn: How to prompt for coverage of the cases your team consistently misses, and why the second prompt produces structurally different output than the first.
surprise: The second prompt consistently surfaces empty states, error states, and permission issues your team would have caught only in QA — or after launch.
prereqs: A user story in 'As a… I want… So that…' format
tip: Write your user story in the standard 'As a [user], I want [action], so that [outcome]' format before starting — structured input produces structured output.

title: Dual-Audience Rewrite
todo: Pick the problem statement or proposed solution section from a PRD. Ask Claude to rewrite it twice: once for 'a skeptical senior engineer focused on technical feasibility and implementation risk' and once for 'a business stakeholder who cares only about outcomes and has no interest in implementation details.'
why: PMs carry enormous communication overhead translating the same ideas for different audiences. This collapses hours of rewriting into minutes.
learn: How to produce audience-specific communication from a single source document — and what each audience actually needs to hear.
surprise: The two outputs will be dramatically different in language, emphasis, and length. The contrast reveals how much your original version was written for neither audience.
prereqs: An existing PRD section to work from
tip: Choose a section that has both technical and business implications — purely technical or purely business sections will produce less interesting contrast.

title: One-Pager Compressor
todo: Paste a strategy doc, research report, or long PRD (1,500+ words) into Claude. Prompt: 'Summarise this as a one-pager for a CEO with 3 minutes. Include: (1) the decision being requested, (2) the top 3 risks, (3) what happens if we do nothing.'
why: AI handles information compression better and faster than most humans. The structured output constraints force the right prioritisation.
learn: How to use constrained output prompts to produce executive-ready summaries, and why 'what happens if we do nothing' is the most important missing section in most strategy docs.
surprise: The 'what happens if we do nothing' constraint generates the sharpest insight in the summary — and it's almost always the section that was missing from the original document.
prereqs: A document of 1,500+ words to compress
tip: Paste the full document — Claude's compression quality degrades if you pre-summarise before pasting.

title: Decision Log Builder
todo: Describe a product decision you made recently: what you decided, why, what alternatives you rejected, and what assumptions you're relying on. Ask Claude to format it as a structured decision log, then ask: 'What are 3 future events that would prove this decision was wrong?'
why: Decision logging is universally recommended and almost never done because it feels like administrative overhead. AI makes it instant.
learn: How to create falsifiable decision records — the foundation of a learning organisation — and why naming 'what would prove us wrong' is the most valuable part.
surprise: The 'what would prove this wrong' prompt forces you to name falsifiable assumptions. Most teams make decisions based on assumptions they never articulate — this makes them explicit while there's still time to act.
tip: Include the alternatives you rejected, not just the decision you made — the rejected paths are often the most revealing part of a decision log.

title: Synthetic User Interview
todo: Write a detailed user persona: job title, company type, size, goals, frustrations. Ask Claude to play that user. Conduct a 15-minute interview about your product concept — ask about pain points, current workarounds, and what they would pay to fix. Treat it as a real interview.
why: Synthetic interviews are not a replacement for real ones — but they're a valuable pre-test that surfaces angles you haven't considered before going to real users.
learn: How to use AI simulation to sharpen your research questions, and the difference between validating assumptions and discovering unknown unknowns.
surprise: Claude as a user will push back on your assumptions in ways real users often don't in early interviews — it's less polite and more direct. This makes it a better preparation tool than you'd expect.
tip: Write the persona description in detail before starting the conversation — include company size, role, goals, and one specific frustration. The richer the persona, the more useful the interview.

title: Survey Response Clustering
todo: Paste 30–50 open-text survey responses or NPS verbatims into Claude. Prompt: 'Cluster these into 5 insight themes, rank by frequency, and give me one representative quote per theme. Then tell me what theme surprised you most and why.'
why: Qualitative data coding takes hours manually. AI compresses it to seconds — and the themes it finds often differ from the ones you assumed going in.
learn: How to use AI for thematic analysis on unstructured text, and how to spot the difference between your confirmation bias and what the data actually says.
surprise: The clusters Claude finds will often differ from the ones you assumed before reading. It catches subtle themes that manual reading misses because your attention is directed by your hypotheses.
prereqs: 30+ open-text survey responses or NPS verbatims
tip: Paste raw, uncleaned responses — resisting the urge to pre-process produces more honest clusters.

title: Hidden Need Surfacing
todo: Paste customer feature requests, support tickets, or interview quotes into Claude. Prompt: 'For each of these, identify: (1) what the customer thinks they want, (2) what problem they are actually trying to solve, (3) what a better solution might look like that they haven't considered.'
why: Feature requests describe solutions, not problems. This prompt operationalises Jobs-to-be-Done thinking in minutes without a formal workshop.
learn: How to extract underlying needs from surface-level requests — the foundational product discovery skill — and how to use AI to do it at volume.
surprise: The gap between 'what they asked for' and 'what they need' is almost always larger than you expect. At least one request will look completely different after this exercise — and become more valuable, not less.
prereqs: 10+ customer quotes, tickets, or feature requests
tip: Include verbatim customer language rather than your interpretation of it — the specific words customers use carry information that paraphrasing loses.

title: JTBD Switch Interview
todo: Ask Claude to play a current user of your product who is seriously considering switching to a competitor. Conduct a Jobs-to-be-Done switch interview: ask about the push driving them away, the pull toward the alternative, the anxieties about switching, and what would make them stay.
why: Switch interviews are the gold standard for understanding churn — and they're expensive and hard to schedule with real users who've already churned.
learn: The JTBD switch interview framework and how to use AI simulation to diagnose retention risk before it shows up in your data.
surprise: Claude as a churning user will articulate frustrations that are uncomfortably accurate. This exercise often surfaces retention risks the team has been aware of but never formally named.
tip: Ask Claude to introduce itself as the user persona and describe their current situation before you start the interview — grounding the conversation in a specific context produces richer responses.

title: User Misunderstanding Predictor
todo: Paste the copy from a new feature announcement, onboarding screen, or marketing page into Claude. Prompt: 'What will first-time users misunderstand about this? Generate the 5 most likely incorrect mental models a new user will form after reading this, and explain why each misunderstanding is likely.'
why: PMs write copy for users who think like them. Getting AI predictions of misunderstanding changes how you write copy before anything ships.
learn: How to use AI to simulate naïve user comprehension — catching copy problems before usability testing rather than during.
surprise: At least 2 of the 5 misunderstandings will match things your support team has already seen. This exercise makes the connection between copy choices and support volume explicit.
prereqs: Copy text that users will actually see
tip: Paste the exact text users will see — not a description of it. The specific words matter.

title: Adversarial User Persona
todo: Ask Claude to create a detailed persona of a user who will deliberately misuse, game, or stress-test your product. Then ask: 'How would this person exploit the system in ways your product team has not anticipated? List the top 3 risks and which existing features are most vulnerable.'
why: Most personas represent aspirational users. The adversarial persona surfaces abuse cases, edge conditions, and trust issues that standard personas completely miss.
learn: How to use adversarial thinking to pressure-test product design — a technique security teams use that almost no PM team applies to UX.
surprise: The adversarial persona will surface at least one exploit your team will immediately recognise as a real problem — and probably already has a support ticket about.
tip: Focus on one specific feature or flow rather than the whole product — the more specific, the more actionable the risk analysis.

title: App Store Review Intelligence
todo: Go to the App Store or Google Play and copy 50+ reviews for your main competitor (sort by Most Recent). Paste them into Claude. Prompt: 'Cluster by theme, rank by frequency, identify the single most common unmet need, and include 3 representative quotes.'
why: App store reviews are free, public, and almost completely ignored by product teams. They're the most accessible form of competitor customer discovery.
learn: How to mine unstructured review data for product intelligence, and why 'most recent' sorting matters for getting current pain points.
surprise: The most frequent complaint is rarely the most-discussed topic in your competitive research. Reviews often surface a pain your competitor's sales team is actively hiding from their own roadmap conversations.
prereqs: 50+ competitor reviews copied from App Store or Play Store
tip: Sort by Most Recent before copying — older reviews reflect a different product and can skew the themes significantly.

title: Deep Research Competitor Report
todo: Open Gemini Advanced. Prompt: 'Produce a competitive landscape report for [your product category]. Include: a feature comparison matrix, pricing model comparison, which customer segment each player is winning, and the #1 gap that none of them currently address well.'
why: A multi-page analyst-quality competitive report used to require a research firm or a week of manual work. Gemini Deep Research produces it in minutes.
learn: How to use AI for systematic competitive analysis rather than ad-hoc searching, and how to prompt for the gap analysis that makes it actionable.
surprise: The 'gap none of them address' section is consistently the most valuable output — and it's almost never the gap your internal team has been talking about.
tip: Name your product category specifically rather than your product name — 'B2B project management for construction teams' beats 'tools like Procore.'

title: Competitor Review Mining
todo: Open Perplexity. Ask: 'Find and synthesise the top complaints about [competitor name] from G2, Reddit, and Trustpilot published in the last 12 months. Format as a table: complaint theme, approximate frequency, severity (high/medium/low), and one representative user quote.'
why: Review sites contain unfiltered customer frustration that competitors' marketing will never acknowledge. Perplexity aggregates them without you visiting each site.
learn: How to use Perplexity for cross-platform sentiment aggregation, and how to prompt for structured output from an open-ended research query.
surprise: Perplexity surfaces specific, recent friction points — not generic complaints. You'll often find a pain your sales team has heard anecdotally but your product team has never formally investigated.
tip: Name the competitor specifically by their company or product name — vague category terms produce less focused results.

title: Competitor Changelog Decoder
todo: Find your competitor's public changelog, release notes, or product blog. Copy entries from the last 6 months. Paste into Claude. Prompt: 'Based on this release history, what is the underlying product strategy? What strategic bet are they making? Which customer segment are they prioritising and why?'
why: Changelogs are completely unanalysed competitive intelligence hiding in plain sight. Every release is a revealed preference about where a company is investing.
learn: How to reverse-engineer product strategy from release history — a technique used by senior product leaders that almost no PM teams practise.
surprise: The pattern across 6 months of releases reveals a strategic direction that's usually invisible in any single announcement. This technique often surfaces competitive moves months before they become obvious.
prereqs: 6 months of competitor release notes or changelog entries
tip: Copy text entries rather than screenshots — Claude processes structured text far more accurately than image-based content.

title: Job Posting Intelligence
todo: Ask Perplexity: 'Find the most recent 8–10 job postings from [competitor]. Synthesise them: what do these hiring priorities reveal about their strategic bets and product direction for the next 12 months? What roles are new versus replacements?'
why: Job postings are a lagging-but-honest signal of investment — companies hire for where they're going, not where they are.
learn: How to read hiring patterns as forward-looking strategic intelligence — a well-known analyst technique that almost no product teams use systematically.
surprise: This technique often reveals investment areas your competitor hasn't publicly announced. New engineering roles in a specific domain are often 6–12 months ahead of any product announcement.
tip: Search for the company name in quotes to get precise results — without quotes, Perplexity may pull competitor listings from similar companies.

title: YouTube Keynote Analyser
todo: Find a competitor's product keynote or conference talk on YouTube. Paste the URL into Gemini. Prompt: 'What is the core product strategy revealed in this video? What customer pain points are they explicitly naming? What features are being prioritised and what does that reveal about their target customer?'
why: Gemini processes YouTube video directly. A 45-minute keynote becomes a 5-minute strategic brief — and keynote language reveals target customer priorities that product pages obscure.
learn: How to use multimodal AI to extract strategic intelligence from video content at scale.
surprise: Keynotes are scripted to sell, but the language used to describe problems reveals which customers they're actually targeting — and that audience is often not who you assumed.
prereqs: A competitor keynote or conference talk on YouTube
tip: Paste the full YouTube URL — shortened links (youtu.be) sometimes fail to load in Gemini.

title: CPO Adversarial Prompt
todo: After reviewing any competitive analysis, prompt Claude: 'Now act as the CPO of [competitor name]. You have just seen our product roadmap for the next 6 months. What specific gaps would you exploit? What is your counter-move, and how quickly could you execute it?'
why: Competitive analysis is almost always done from your own perspective. Forcing AI into your competitor's mindset produces structurally different and more useful insights.
learn: How adversarial role-playing surfaces strategic blind spots that conventional analysis misses — and why the counter-move framing matters.
surprise: Claude will identify leverage points against your roadmap that are uncomfortably specific. At least one will be a risk your team has discussed informally but never formally addressed.
prereqs: Your roadmap or upcoming initiative list
tip: Name the specific competitor, not 'our competitor' — Claude produces more specific and useful adversarial analysis when it can ground it in a real company's known positioning.

title: Competitor Pricing Deconstruction
todo: Go to a competitor's pricing page. Copy the full page text (or screenshot it and upload). Prompt Claude: 'What psychological pricing techniques are being used here? What customer segment is each tier designed to capture? What is the decoy tier, and what is missing from this pricing model?'
why: PMs study competitor pricing visually but rarely deconstruct the psychological architecture — anchoring, decoy pricing, loss aversion — that drives conversion.
learn: Pricing psychology fundamentals and how to read competitor tier design as a strategic signal about which segments they're fighting for.
surprise: The 'what's missing' question often reveals a segment your competitor has deliberately left underserved — which may be your best wedge into the market.
prereqs: Competitor pricing page URL or screenshot
tip: Include the full pricing page including the FAQs — pricing page FAQ language reveals the objections the company hears most often.

title: Pre-Mortem Generator
todo: Describe an upcoming launch, initiative, or major decision in 3–4 sentences. Prompt ChatGPT: 'This initiative has just failed. It is 6 months from now and the post-mortem is happening. Explain exactly how it failed — what went wrong, in what order, and what the early warning signs were that everyone ignored.'
why: A narrative failure story surfaces cascade failures and second-order consequences that a standard risk list completely misses.
learn: Pre-mortem methodology — the most evidence-backed technique for identifying project risks before they happen.
surprise: The narrative framing forces specificity that a bullet-pointed risk register never achieves. At least one failure mode will be something your team hasn't formally discussed — and should discuss before starting.
tip: Include the timeline and key stakeholders involved — the more specific the context, the more specific and useful the failure narrative.

title: TAM Model Builder
todo: Describe your product and target customer in 3–4 sentences, including your price point and typical sales cycle. Prompt Claude: 'Build a bottom-up TAM/SAM/SOM model. Show your math, make every assumption explicit, and tell me what would have to be true to make the TAM 2x larger.'
why: Market sizing is routinely outsourced to bizops or skipped entirely. Claude produces a defensible first draft with exposed assumptions in minutes.
learn: How to construct a bottom-up market sizing model, what makes one defensible, and why the expansion question produces better strategic insight than the base model.
surprise: The 'what would make it 2x larger' constraint generates better thinking about expansion vectors than the base model itself — it forces strategic reasoning that most sizing exercises skip.
tip: Include your price point and sales cycle length — without these, Claude can only produce a top-down estimate, which is far less useful.

title: Scenario Planning Sprint
todo: Write one strategic bet your team is currently making as a single clear sentence. Ask Claude to generate three scenarios for 18 months from now: optimistic (bet pays off), base case (mixed results), and black-swan disruption (the bet is made irrelevant). For each, describe what the product must look like to succeed.
why: Scenario planning is taught in every strategy course and practised by almost no PM teams — because it feels like a multi-day workshop. AI makes it a solo 30-minute exercise.
learn: How to use scenario thinking to stress-test strategic assumptions and what 'pre-mortem for strategy' looks like in practice.
surprise: The black-swan scenario almost always features a disruption your team has privately worried about but never formally discussed. Naming it explicitly creates the conversation your team needs to have.
tip: Write the strategic bet as one precise sentence before starting — 'enterprise is our growth vector for the next 18 months' is more useful than a paragraph.

title: Stakeholder Gauntlet
todo: Describe a product decision you need executive buy-in for, including the business context (budget, timeline, team size). Prompt ChatGPT: 'Generate the 5 hardest questions a skeptical CFO, a cautious Head of Engineering, and a VP of International Sales would ask about this. Then give me strong, specific answers to each question.'
why: PMs mentally simulate stakeholder conversations but consistently miss obvious objections. AI plays devil's advocate without the social friction of a real colleague.
learn: How to stress-test a business case from multiple stakeholder perspectives before the meeting — and what each function actually cares about.
surprise: At least 2 of the generated questions will be ones you don't have a good answer to yet. Finding that out now is dramatically better than finding it out in the room.
tip: Include the budget, timeline, and team size in your description — without business context, the questions generated will be generic rather than specific to your situation.

title: Roadmap Single Point of Failure
todo: Paste your roadmap or describe your top 5 upcoming initiatives. Prompt Claude: 'Identify dependencies across these initiatives. Which single team, workstream, or external dependency, if delayed, would derail the most other items? What are the early warning signs that this is about to happen?'
why: Dependency mapping in Jira is painful and rarely maintained. AI reading plain-English descriptions catches different — and often more important — risks than a formal dependency matrix.
learn: How to identify critical path dependencies and concentration risk in a product roadmap, and how to convert dependency risk into a concrete monitoring plan.
surprise: The 'early warning signs' output is usually the most actionable part — it turns an abstract dependency risk into specific things to watch for, which most teams never do.
prereqs: Your roadmap or a list of upcoming initiatives
tip: Include estimated timelines if you have them — even rough ones significantly improve the quality of the dependency analysis.

title: Metric Definition Audit
todo: Describe exactly how your team calculates your most important product metric — what counts as an event, what the denominator is, how edge cases are handled. Prompt Claude: 'Give me 3 specific ways this metric definition could be actively misleading us. For each, describe the bad product decision we might make because of it.'
why: Metric definitions are set early and rarely revisited. Bad metrics drive bad priorities for months or years before anyone connects the dots.
learn: How to audit measurement design for common failure modes — proxy metric drift, denominator problems, survivorship bias — and why definition precision matters.
surprise: At least one of the three misleading scenarios will describe a situation your team has actually been in — and blamed on something else entirely.
tip: Describe exactly how you calculate the metric — what counts as an 'activation event', what the denominator includes — without this precision, Claude can only give generic feedback.

title: CSV Insight Sprint
todo: Export a real CSV from your product analytics, NPS tool, or CRM. Upload it to ChatGPT. Prompt: 'What are the 5 most interesting patterns in this data? Do not tell me what I already know. Then tell me what you would investigate further, and why.'
why: PMs who can't write SQL can now get analyst-quality pattern spotting directly. The 'do not tell me what I already know' constraint forces non-obvious findings.
learn: How to use AI data analysis for exploratory analysis on raw data, and why unguided exploration often finds different things than hypothesis-driven analysis.
surprise: The 'what would you investigate further' prompt generates better follow-up questions than most analysts write themselves — because it's not anchored to your existing hypotheses.
prereqs: ChatGPT Plus (paid — required for file upload)
A CSV export from your analytics tool
tip: Export your data as-is without cleaning — messy real data produces more honest and useful analysis than curated data.

title: Cohort Comparison Without SQL
todo: Export a CSV with user signup dates and engagement or activation events. Upload it to ChatGPT. Prompt: 'Compare the behaviour of users who signed up in the first half of this year versus the second half. What is the single biggest behavioural difference, and what might explain it?'
why: Cohort analysis is a standard data team request with a 2-day SLA. This makes it a 20-minute self-serve exercise — and you can do it between meetings.
learn: How to perform cohort-based analysis using natural language, and why self-serve analysis changes which questions you ask (because the cost of asking is near zero).
surprise: The insight that surfaces is almost never the one you would have requested. AI-directed exploratory analysis finds different patterns than hypothesis-driven analysis because it has no prior beliefs to confirm.
prereqs: ChatGPT Plus (paid — required for file upload)
CSV with user signup dates and event data
tip: Make sure your CSV has clear column headers — ChatGPT uses them to understand what each field represents.

title: Chart Interpretation Challenge
todo: Take a screenshot of a confusing analytics dashboard, a graph from a data review, or a visualisation you don't fully understand. Upload it to Gemini. Prompt: 'Explain what this chart is showing, what is concerning about it, and what action it implies I should take.'
why: PMs stare at dashboards without asking 'so what.' Having AI narrate a chart's implications — including the uncomfortable ones — builds analytical muscle.
learn: How to use multimodal AI to interpret data visualisations and connect them to decisions — the 'so what' skill that separates data-fluent PMs.
surprise: Gemini will often name an implication you noticed but mentally set aside. Externalising it creates the conversation your team needs to have.
prereqs: A screenshot of a chart or dashboard
tip: Crop the screenshot to just the chart — removing surrounding dashboard UI helps Gemini focus on the data rather than the interface.

title: Feedback-to-Roadmap Bridge
todo: Paste 20+ support tickets, G2 reviews, or user feedback items into Claude. Prompt: 'What are the top 5 product changes that would eliminate the most complaints? For each, estimate effort (low/medium/high) and potential impact, and explain your reasoning.'
why: Support data is rich product intelligence that rarely gets structured into roadmap input. This converts unstructured qualitative noise into a prioritised roadmap input in minutes.
learn: How to convert unstructured qualitative data into a prioritised input for the roadmap process, and how effort/impact framing forces useful trade-off thinking.
surprise: The highest-impact, lowest-effort change identified is almost never what leadership assumes it is. This output is worth bringing directly into your next roadmap review — it tends to shift the conversation.
prereqs: 20+ support tickets, reviews, or feedback items
tip: Paste raw support tickets or reviews rather than already-processed themes — the raw language carries signals that summarised versions lose.

title: Slack Thread Distiller
todo: Find a long Slack thread (30+ messages) where a decision was discussed but not cleanly resolved. Copy the full thread including usernames. Prompt Claude: 'Extract: (1) the core decision being discussed, (2) who disagrees and their main objection, (3) what is still unresolved, (4) what the next concrete action should be.'
why: Teams lose enormous time re-reading long threads trying to reconstruct alignment. Structured extraction turns chaos into clarity in seconds.
learn: How to use AI to create decision structure from unstructured asynchronous conversation — and why the 'what is unresolved' output is the most valuable.
surprise: The 'what is still unresolved' output usually names the thing everyone in the thread was dancing around but avoiding saying directly. It forces the real conversation.
tip: Include usernames when you paste — Claude can then attribute disagreements to specific people, which makes the output dramatically more useful for follow-up.

title: Exec Presentation Anticipator
todo: Paste a strategy doc or PRD. Prompt ChatGPT: 'Summarise this for the exec team in 3 slides (slide titles and 3 bullet points each). Then generate 5 hard questions they are likely to ask, and write strong, specific answers to each question.'
why: Combining summary and Q&A prep in one exercise is faster than doing them separately — and catches more gaps because the summary forces you to see what's missing.
learn: How to reverse-engineer the questions a senior audience will ask from a written document, and why writing answers before the meeting changes how you present.
surprise: At least 2 of the 5 generated questions will be ones you don't have a good answer for yet. It's always better to find that out before the room does.
prereqs: A strategy doc or PRD to work from
tip: Include the decision you are asking the exec team to make — without this, the generated questions will be informational rather than decision-focused.

title: A/B Copy Blitz
todo: Write one headline or value proposition for a feature launch, email, or landing page. Ask Claude to generate 8 variations across different emotional tones: curiosity, urgency, social proof, aspiration, fear of missing out, simplicity, authority, and humour. Pick your top 3 for real testing.
why: PMs and PMMs typically generate 2–3 copy variants because writing more feels slow. Getting 8 in 60 seconds reframes what 'testing' even means.
learn: How emotional framing changes copywriting effectiveness and how to use AI to rapidly explore a copy space before committing to testing.
surprise: At least one of the 8 variants will be in a tone you would never have tried yourself — and it will often be the most compelling one.
tip: Write your original headline before asking for variations — having a baseline makes it much easier to identify what's working and what isn't in the alternatives.

title: Release Notes in 3 Tones
todo: Describe a feature you're shipping in 2–3 sentences. Ask Claude to write release notes in three distinct voices: (1) casual and human for end users, (2) enterprise-formal for procurement and compliance audiences, (3) developer-focused with technical details and integration notes. Compare which fits your brand.
why: Most teams write one version of release notes and send it to all audiences. This makes it a 10-minute exercise to produce the three versions you actually need.
learn: How to produce audience-segmented communications from a single source of truth, and why 'brand voice' is a controllable AI input.
surprise: Reading all three versions side by side reveals which audience your current release notes style is actually written for — and it's usually not your primary user.
tip: Include who each tone is for in your prompt — 'casual = for our consumer app users who are not technical' gives Claude the context to calibrate correctly.

title: Voice Presentation Coach
todo: Open the ChatGPT mobile app and turn on voice mode. Give your product strategy presentation or pitch out loud — as if you're standing in the room. When done, ask for feedback on: what was unclear, what felt like filler, your strongest moment, and your weakest moment.
why: Most PMs rehearse presentations in their heads. Speaking out loud to an AI exposes verbal habits — filler words, rushed explanations, assumption-jumping — that silent rehearsal never catches.
learn: How real-time voice interaction with AI differs from text, and how to use it for communication skill development.
surprise: The feedback will identify the exact moment in your pitch where you lose the room — and it will be the same moment every time. Fixing one thing materially improves the whole presentation.
tip: Use headphones so the mic doesn't pick up the AI's voice during your presentation — it causes feedback loops that disrupt the session.

title: Build Your PM Assistant
todo: Create a new Claude Project. In the project instructions, write: your product name and what it does, your target users, your 2 main competitors, your team's current top priority, and your preferred output format. Then use it for a real work task and compare the output to a standard Claude conversation.
why: Most PMs use Claude as a stateless chatbot. A configured Project turns it into a context-aware colleague who already knows your world — every conversation starts with full context.
learn: How to configure a persistent AI context, why it produces structurally better outputs than one-off prompts, and what belongs in project instructions versus individual prompts.
surprise: The first response from a well-configured Project will feel like it was written by someone who actually knows your company. The difference versus a blank conversation is immediately, dramatically clear.
prereqs: Claude Pro or Max (Projects feature requires paid plan)
tip: Spend 5 minutes writing the project instructions on paper before creating the project — thinking through what context matters most produces much better instructions than typing directly into the interface.

title: Extended Thinking for Hard Tradeoffs
todo: Identify a genuinely difficult product tradeoff you're currently facing — one your team has been going back and forth on. Enable extended thinking in Claude, frame it as 'should we do X or Y and why', and after reading the response, specifically read the thinking trace to understand how the model reasoned.
why: Extended thinking produces visibly different reasoning. The thinking trace teaches you how the model weighs competing arguments — which is often more useful than the final answer.
learn: How extended thinking differs from standard generation, how to evaluate whether AI reasoning is sound, and how visible reasoning changes how you use the output.
surprise: The thinking trace considers and discards arguments you hadn't thought of. Reading it reveals the structure of the decision space — and often the argument the model almost made but didn't.
prereqs: Claude Pro or Max (extended thinking requires paid plan)
Extended thinking toggle enabled in model selector
tip: Frame the tradeoff as a binary 'should we do X or Y' rather than an open question — binary framing produces more structured and useful extended reasoning.

title: NotebookLM Research Podcast
todo: Create a new NotebookLM notebook. Upload 4–5 sources: competitor blog posts, analyst reports, or product teardowns. Generate an Audio Overview. Listen to it during a walk or commute and note any implications or tensions the AI hosts surface that you hadn't considered.
why: Dense research documents are hard to absorb by reading. Two AI voices debating the material surface implications and tensions that solo reading misses.
learn: How to use multimodal AI to convert written research into listenable, discussion-format content — and why the debate structure is cognitively different from reading.
surprise: The audio format forces the material to argue with itself — which is how you spot the weak assumptions in competitive research that feel solid on paper.
tip: Upload all sources before generating the Audio Overview — adding sources after generation requires a new overview.

title: Screenshot UX Audit
todo: Take screenshots of your product's current onboarding flow (3–5 consecutive screens). Upload all of them in one Gemini message. Prompt: 'Critique the UX of this onboarding flow. What would a first-time user be confused by at each step? What would a senior UX designer flag immediately?'
why: A formal UX audit requires a designer and a usability lab. This makes it a solo 15-minute PM activity you can do before any design review.
learn: How to use multimodal AI for rapid UX evaluation, and how the 'senior UX designer' framing produces structurally different feedback than asking 'what's wrong.'
surprise: The issues identified will overlap significantly with your next usability test findings. This exercise often surfaces the same things a $5,000 usability study would find — in 15 minutes.
tip: Take screenshots at the same browser zoom level across all screens — inconsistent zoom makes it harder for Gemini to assess visual hierarchy and text readability.

title: Multimodal Competitive UX Teardown
todo: Take screenshots of the same flow in your product and a competitor's product (same stage — both onboarding, or both checkout, or both core feature). Upload all screenshots in one Gemini message. Prompt: 'Compare the UX of these two flows. Which is better and why? List 3 specific UI changes that would close the gap.'
why: Visual UX comparison has historically required a designer. This makes it a solo PM activity that produces specific, actionable design input.
learn: How to frame visual comparison prompts to get specific, actionable feedback — and what 'close the gap' framing does versus 'what's wrong.'
surprise: The comparison will often surface a gap you knew existed but hadn't articulated specifically enough to put on the roadmap. Now you have the language — and a concrete change to prioritise.
prereqs: Screenshots of the same flow in your product and a competitor's product
tip: Use the same flow in both products for a fair comparison — comparing your onboarding to their core feature produces noise, not signal.

title: PRD-to-Tickets in Cursor
todo: Open Cursor. Create a new markdown file and paste your PRD into it. Open the chat panel and ask: 'Break this PRD into Jira-style tickets with acceptance criteria. Flag anywhere the PRD is ambiguous, under-specified, or contradicts itself.'
why: Cursor operates inside your file context — it can cross-reference your spec and flag internal contradictions automatically, which a standalone AI tool cannot do.
learn: How to use AI-native editors as PM productivity tools beyond their intended coding use case — and why file context changes the quality of AI analysis.
surprise: The 'flag ambiguity' instruction will return real issues your engineering team would have raised in sprint planning — but now you find them before the meeting, when they're cheap to fix.
prereqs: Your PRD as a text or markdown file
tip: Create the markdown file and paste your PRD before opening the chat — Cursor analyses the active file, not content you paste into the chat.

title: Research Digest Agent
todo: Build a Zapier Agent that monitors 2–3 competitor blogs or news sources via RSS. Configure it to summarise new posts and send a weekly Slack or email digest. Start with one source, confirm it works, then add the others. Total build time: 20–30 minutes, no code required.
why: PMs manually check competitor blogs and miss most updates. One afternoon of agent-building eliminates this work permanently.
learn: How to build a no-code monitoring agent, what 'agentic' automation actually means in practice, and how to think about building agents that run without you.
surprise: The setup is simpler than you expect. The bigger surprise is realising how much competitive intelligence you've been missing by doing this manually — the agent will surface things your weekly competitor check missed.
prereqs: Zapier account (free tier may work for basic setup)
Competitor RSS feed URLs or blog URLs
tip: Start with just one source and confirm the digest arrives correctly before adding more — debugging one connection at a time is far easier than debugging three simultaneously.

title: Meeting Notes-to-Tickets Agent
todo: Build a Zapier workflow: when a new document is created in your meeting notes folder (Notion or Google Docs), send the content to ChatGPT via Zapier to extract action items, then automatically create tickets in Jira or Linear with those action items. Test it with a real meeting note.
why: The gap between 'we talked about it in standup' and 'it exists in Jira' is where initiatives disappear. Automating the handoff eliminates a consistent source of dropped work.
learn: How to chain AI with workflow automation tools to eliminate manual handoffs — and how to think about where human review belongs in an automated workflow.
surprise: The build takes under 30 minutes — and then it runs forever. The ratio of setup time to time saved is unlike almost any other productivity investment you'll make.
prereqs: Notion or Google Docs for meeting notes
tip: Test with a simple, short meeting note first before connecting to your live projects — it's much easier to debug the extraction prompt on a controlled input.

title: Weekly Avoidance Prompt
todo: Every Monday morning, paste your task list and write one sentence about what you're currently working on. Prompt ChatGPT: 'Based on this task list, what is the one thing I am probably avoiding this week? What is the smallest possible first step that would make it feel less avoidable?'
why: PM task lists are full of deferred hard decisions disguised as to-dos. This prompt identifies the real blocker — the decision or conversation you're not having.
learn: How to use AI as a lightweight self-coaching tool for managing avoidance in knowledge work, and why the smallest-step framing matters for activation energy.
surprise: The identified avoidance is almost always correct. The model reads between the lines of a task list and names the thing you already know — but haven't admitted to yourself yet. Most people find this uncomfortable in a useful way.
tip: Do this before reading emails or Slack on Monday — doing it after means your task list reflects what others want from you rather than what you're actually responsible for.
